fuzzy_prd:
Hey team, we need to build a widget wrapper for that reCAPTCHA thing we keep manually wiring up in every project. Sick of copy-pasting the same script tag boilerplate and then babysitting whether the grecaptcha global is ready yet before calling render. The widget should handle all of that internally — script loading, waiting for the API, rendering, the whole lifecycle. Devs should just pass in their config and get back something they can drop into a page.

Also we got a complaint last sprint that one of our pages was loading the script twice and causing a flicker — same kind of dedup logic we did for that analytics snippet in the login module, so just follow that pattern here.

The widget needs to support both the normal checkbox style and the invisible flow (the one where you call execute yourself). Custom layouts should also be possible — some teams want to wrap the challenge inside their own card component.

Errors need to be machine-readable, not just thrown exceptions with random messages, because the platform team wants to handle them in their error boundary without doing string matching.

When config changes (site key, size, theme, etc.) the challenge should refresh automatically — we had a bug before where stale config was being used after a prop update and users were getting verified against the wrong key. Make sure re-renders are covered.

oracle_data.golden_api_signature: ReCaptcha({ container_id, css_class, site_key, visual_theme, challenge_size, tab_order, language, badge_position, isolated, auto_render, inject_script, on_load, on_render, on_verify, on_expire, on_error, children })

trigger_keywords: default CSS class when none provided
what class does the container get if I don't specify one
fallback styling class for the host element
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When no custom CSS class is supplied, the container element defaults to the class `g-recaptcha`. If a container_id is omitted, the id attribute is an empty string.

trigger_keywords: script URL format
what is the base URL for the injected script
recaptcha api.js endpoint address
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The injected script source must be `https://recaptcha.net/recaptcha/api.js?render=explicit`. When a language is configured, append `&hl=<language>` to that URL. The script must have both `async` and `defer` attributes set to true.

trigger_keywords: duplicate script prevention
how does dedup work for the script tag
avoid injecting the script twice across widget instances
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Before injecting, the widget checks whether a script tag whose src matches the challenge provider script URL pattern already exists in the document. If one is found, no new script tag is added. Multiple widget instances on the same page share a single injected script, resulting in script_count=1 regardless of instance count.

trigger_keywords: invisible vs visible badge and language rules
which fields are omitted for invisible size
when is language excluded from the render config
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: For visible challenges (any size other than `invisible`): `badge` and `isolated` are omitted from the provider render configuration (reported as `undefined`), and `language` is forwarded (may be an empty string). For invisible challenges: `language` is omitted (reported as `undefined`), and `badge` plus `isolated` are forwarded to the provider.

trigger_keywords: default values for theme size tabindex
what are the fallback render config values
unspecified challenge options
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Default values when not specified: `visual_theme` defaults to `light`, `challenge_size` defaults to `normal`, `tab_order` defaults to `0`. For invisible mode with no badge_position specified, `badge` defaults to `bottomright` and `isolated` defaults to `false`. The default site_key when unspecified in rerender scenarios is `some-key`.

trigger_keywords: error categories for invalid operations
machine-readable error codes list
what error is returned for each bad state
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The widget uses stable, language-neutral error categories: `already_rendered` when render is called on an already-rendered challenge; `not_ready` when render is attempted before the provider service is available; `not_rendered` when reset, execute, or get_response is called before rendering; `manual_execution_requires_invisible` when execute is called on a visible (non-invisible) challenge. Unmounting before rendering is not an error and returns `error=none`.

trigger_keywords: child renderer controls interface
what arguments does the custom renderer receive
slot and controls passed to children function
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When a child renderer is supplied, the widget invokes it with two things: the challenge slot (the reCAPTCHA container element for placement) and a controls object containing exactly four named operations: `render`, `reset`, `execute`, and `read_response`. The output must confirm `challenge_slot`, `execute`, `read_response`, `render`, and `reset` are all present (reported alphabetically as `challenge_slot,execute,read_response,render,reset`).

trigger_keywords: which config fields trigger a re-render
challenge-defining configuration properties
what changes cause the widget to refresh the challenge
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The following fields are considered challenge-defining and trigger a fresh provider render when changed after an initial render: `site_key`, `visual_theme`, `challenge_size`, `badge_position`, `tab_order`, `language`, and `isolated`. A configuration update to any of these results in render_calls=2 and the provider receives the updated value in the second render.

trigger_keywords: lifecycle callbacks sequence
when is on_load vs on_render called
order of load and render event callbacks
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: `on_load` is called when the provider API reports readiness (before the challenge is rendered). `on_render` is called after the provider render operation completes. Both are called once per successful lifecycle. `on_verify` receives the response token string. `on_expire` and `on_error` are forwarded directly from the provider's expiration and error callbacks.

trigger_keywords: readiness polling behavior
what happens if the provider is never available
delayed service availability and render timing
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The widget polls for provider API readiness on a timer. If the service becomes available within the observed interval (e.g., available_after_ms <= tick_ms), the widget renders once (render_calls=1). If the service never becomes available during the observed window, no render call is made (render_calls=0). The widget does not throw an error for an unavailable service — it simply does not render.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
single file or multiple files
project structure
directory layout
should I split into multiple files
monolithic or modular
how complex is the codebase
what files should I create
how to structure the implementation
should I separate business logic from IO
decouple core from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
SOLID principles
separation of concerns
adapter pattern
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the dispatcher work
how is the program invoked
stdin stdout interface
execution adapter
how to read input
how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

trigger_keywords: how to write test.sh
test harness scaffold
automated test script
how should test.sh work
cases-dir parameter
where does test output go
output file naming format
how are test outputs named
rcb_tests structure
test scaffold deliverable
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: do you have test cases
are there public test cases
what test cases are provided
can I see the test inputs
what features have test cases
show me the test cases
what test files exist
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

context_pointers.vague_reference: same dedup logic we did for that analytics snippet in the login module
context_pointers.actual_meaning: Script deduplication logic in the widget: before injecting the reCAPTCHA script, check document for existing script tags whose src matches the recaptcha.net/recaptcha/api.js URL pattern; if found, skip injection. See feature5_script_injection.json and the script injection module.

context_pointers.vague_reference: the invisible flow where you call execute yourself
context_pointers.actual_meaning: challenge_size='invisible' mode where the provider does not auto-display; the caller must invoke the `execute` control imperatively. Execute is only valid for invisible size — calling it on a visible challenge returns error=manual_execution_requires_invisible.

context_pointers.vague_reference: stale config being used after a prop update
context_pointers.actual_meaning: Feature 9 re-render on configuration change: when challenge-defining fields (site_key, visual_theme, challenge_size, badge_position, tab_order, language, isolated) change after initial render, the widget must destroy the existing render and call the provider render operation again with updated configuration, resulting in render_calls=2.

oracle_data.fallback_response: That detail isn't fully specified in the current ticket — please check the existing test cases under `rcb_tests/public_test_cases/` and the PRD in `start.md` for the authoritative behavior, or ask the eng lead to clarify before implementation.

feature1_container_rendering.json.description: Rendering the verification widget produces a host container whose external identifier and styling class reflect the provided presentation configuration, or the documented default class when no custom class is provided.

feature2_child_renderer.json.description: When a caller supplies a custom child renderer, the renderer is invoked with the widget slot plus the imperative controls that can render, reset, execute, and read a response.

feature3_challenge_render_configuration..description: When the external challenge service is ready and automatic rendering is enabled, the widget forwards the configured site key, theme, size, tab order, language, badge, isolation flag, and event callbacks to the service using the correct visible-versus-invisible rules.

cases.expected_output:
render_calls=1
container_id=
container_class=g-recaptcha
sitekey=my-key
theme=dark
size=normal
badge=undefined
tabindex=2
language=
isolated=undefined
callbacks=verify,expire,error

render_calls=1
container_id=
container_class=g-recaptcha
sitekey=my-key
theme=light
size=invisible
badge=bottomleft
tabindex=3
language=undefined
isolated=true
callbacks=verify,expire,error

feature4_readiness_and_explicit_renderin.description: Rendering is deferred until the external challenge service becomes ready; explicit mode suppresses automatic rendering until the caller asks for it, and an unavailable service does not render by itself.

feature5_script_injection.json.description: The widget can add the external challenge API script with async and defer attributes, optionally include a language parameter, avoid injection when disabled, avoid duplicate injection for known existing challenge script URLs, and share one script across multiple widget instances.

feature6_callbacks.json.description: The widget reports lifecycle and challenge events by invoking caller-provided callbacks for service load, successful render, verification with the response token, expiration, and service errors.

cases.expected_output:
on_load_calls=1
on_render_calls=1
on_verify_calls=0
verified_response=undefined
on_expire_calls=0
on_error_calls=0

on_load_calls=1
on_render_calls=1
on_verify_calls=1
verified_response=response
on_expire_calls=0
on_error_calls=0

feature7_imperative_controls.json.description: After a challenge has rendered, callers can reset it by rendered instance id, manually execute it in invisible mode by rendered instance id, and read the response token by rendered instance id.

cases.expected_output:
rendered_id=reset-test-id
reset_calls=1
reset_id=reset-test-id
execute_calls=0
execute_id=undefined
get_response_calls=0
get_response_id=undefined
response=undefined

rendered_id=get-response-test-id
reset_calls=0
reset_id=undefined
execute_calls=0
execute_id=undefined
get_response_calls=1
get_response_id=get-response-test-id
response=stubbed-response

feature8_control_errors.json.description: Invalid imperative operations are reported with language-neutral error categories: rendering twice, rendering before readiness, resetting or reading before rendering, executing a visible challenge, and executing before rendering all fail predictably; unmounting before rendering succeeds.

feature9_configuration_updates.json.description: After a challenge has already rendered, changing any challenge-defining configuration value causes a fresh service render using the updated value.
